title: US-Iran peace deal a blow to the Kurds but their struggle will continue, says Wladimir van Wilgenburg
gist: Podcast episode summary: Trump courted Kurdish support to topple Iran's regime but dropped them under pressure from NATO ally Turkey, and the subsequent US-Iran peace deal leaves Iran's Kurds more isolated; scholar Wladimir van Wilgenburg argues the deal will not end the Kurdish struggle for rights.

themes.theme: Kurds as casualties of the US-Iran deal

insights.claim: Trump sought Kurdish support to help topple Iran's regime but then dropped the Kurds under pressure from NATO ally Turkey, leaving Iran's Kurds more isolated after signing the peace deal with Tehran.
insights.speaker: Wladimir van Wilgenburg
insights.so_what: Shows Turkey's leverage over US Iran policy and that Kurdish allies are expendable bargaining chips — a recurring reliability discount on US-Kurdish partnerships.
insights.quote: Trump sought Kurdish support in efforts to topple Iran's regime, only to drop them like hot potatoes amid pressure from Turkey, a key NATO ally.

insights.claim: Dutch scholar Wladimir van Wilgenburg argues the US-Iran agreement will not stop the Kurds from continuing their struggle for recognition and rights.
insights.speaker: Wladimir van Wilgenburg
insights.so_what: Implies a persistent Kurdish-Iran friction point that the deal does not resolve — a residual instability vector in northern Iran and the wider region.
insights.quote: the agreement will not stop the Kurds from continuing their struggle for recognition and rights
